Explain Type 1 and Type 2 LSAs and describe how they differ.
questions.a: Type 1 (Router LSA) is generated by every OSPF router to describe its directly connected links and is flooded only within its own area. Type 2 (Network LSA) is generated only by the Designated Router (DR) on broadcast networks to describe all routers attached to that segment, and is also flooded only within the area.

questions.q: What is the role of Type 3 and Type 4 LSAs in multi-area OSPF networks?
questions.a: Type 3 (Summary LSA) is generated by the ABR to advertise networks from one area to another. Type 4 (ASBR Summary LSA) is also generated by the ABR, but its purpose is specifically to advertise the route to reach an ASBR (Autonomous System Boundary Router) to other areas.

questions.q: Describe Type 5 LSA and explain when it is generated.
questions.a: Type 5 (External LSA) is generated by an ASBR when routes from an external routing domain (like EIGRP, BGP, or static routes) are redistributed into OSPF. It is flooded throughout the entire OSPF autonomous system (except stub areas).

questions.q: Explain how LSAs are stored and used in the OSPF Link-State Database (LSDB).
questions.a: LSAs are collected and stored in the LSDB, ensuring all routers in the same area have an identical map of the network topology. The SPF (Dijkstra) algorithm then runs against the LSDB to calculate the shortest and best paths to all known destinations.

1. Which OSPF router type connects a backbone area to a non-backbone area?

3. Who is responsible for generating Type 2 (Network) LSAs on a broadcast network?
options: D. Every OSPF router on the segment

questions.q: 4. What is the purpose of a Type 3 (Summary) LSA?
options: A. To advertise external routes from a different AS.
B. To advertise network prefixes from one area into another area via the ABR.
C. To describe the DR on a multi-access segment.
D. To synchronize the LSDB during the Exchange state.

B. It becomes the BDR immediately.
C. It cannot participate in the election and will never be DR or BDR.
D. It only participates if all other routers have a priority of 0.

questions.q: 9. Which OSPF multicast address is used by DROthers to send updates to the DR and BDR?

11. In what order does an OSPF router choose its Router ID (RID)?
options: A. Physical Interface > Loopback > Manual Configuration
B. Manual Configuration > Highest Loopback IP > Highest Physical IP
C. Lowest Physical IP > Highest Loopback IP > Manual Configuration
D. Process ID > Area ID > Priority

questions.q: 12. What OSPF packet type is used to request specific link-state records from a neighbor?
13. Which command is used to restart the OSPF process so that a new Router ID can take effect?
options: D. shutdown followed by no shutdown on the interface

questions.q: 14. Why are OSPF networks divided into areas?
options: A. To increase the size of the Link-State Database (LSDB).
B. To allow different routers to have different views of the topology.
C. To reduce the CPU load and limit the scope of SPF calculations.
D. To enable RIP and OSPF to communicate.
